sentences.text: To get a presidential exemption, a so-called white card, which exempted you from deportation as an economically essential Jew for the Slovak Republic, you had to have a sponsor.

And my father from his days at the-- in the district council became very well acquainted and good friends with two priests who were very influential-- Father Bronecky, the, the Catholic priest and Senior Zeman, the head of the Evangelical Church.
He went to them and said, you know, what can I do?
And Bronecky, who was a good friend and senior to, to [INAUDIBLE], the president, suggested that baptism would greatly improve the chances of, of, of getting a white card.
And that he personally would intervene.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Catholic baptism was very, very difficult to execute.
It took a long time.
So an agreement was reached that an Evangelical baptism of the Lutheran church would be the solution.

And so within three months after bar mitzvah, which I was in January 24, 1942-- within three months, I started studying Catechism in order to prepare for a formal baptism which took place, I believe in June of 1942, in the middle of the deportation.
Within weeks my father received a white card, which protected him and his family, and also applied to his own parents, which was really the key.

Immediately, they changed our position because after the end of the deportations in September of 1942-- cessation of deportation-- the Jewish population in Trencin was brought down to less than 4% of the pre-war population.
And because I was baptized, following procedures, I was able to go back to school.
And so I was admitted to the Catholic school as a student, and was treated just like everybody else.
The prospect of baptism, while deportations take place, take a very simple view.
That you do that because you have to do it.
The alternative is, is, is too bad to contemplate.

And what of a-- what of a apprehension my mother would have had, and particularly my father's parents-- who did not get baptized, by the way-- that was quickly brushed away in the rush to do all the proper things and fill out all the forms.
You must understand, in order to get a white card and to get baptized, it involved an enormous amount of legal rigmarole and procedural rigmarole.
And I, I think that-- I remember it as a fairly smooth procedure.
We, we had to actually go to, to catechism schools and, and study the Christian religion and understand what it says.
And we actually had to pass an examination.
I mean, you must understand that part of the heritage of that whole culture is extreme dominance of the state over private lives.
And so if you follow those procedures you are certified as having done the right thing.
Oh yes, she had to.
Yeah, but she went through the motions because that was the right thing to do.
Oh, many other Jews got baptized too.
And let me just say that in April and May of 1942, you would have signed your blood to the devil.
I mean, you know, if the devil himself would have come and asked you for a [INAUDIBLE], you would have done it.
We did not have to wear a star.
No.
No.
See, baptism was a way of preserving your life.
But all of those other laws still applied to you as a Jew.
I mean, this was a very convenient arrangement.
So you were still subject to all the other restrictions.
The only thing the white card did is you were not deported.
I went back to school because the head of the school was a friend of my father's, and it was a special accommodation.
And he found-- he found a loophole in the law by which said well, you know, if he is baptized he doesn't fall into this category.
In other words, you don't have to fill a form which says Jew, but you could say, he's a Lutheran, you know, then, then you complied with the law.
You must understand this continual legalistic manipulation that takes place though all of this time, looking at the fine details of how to negotiate your way around this or that restriction.
Totally ended.
I must say that the conditions were so depressed and so extreme-- and by the way, then we had to move again, even after the baptism to a slum, to a really slum.
I mean, we were really pushed down into the gutter.

That-- And the scrounging for food and, you know, scrounging for survival was so extreme that the luxury of anything else, even with the possibility of any kind of a betrayal seeing, for instance, lighting the candles on Friday night, just was totally out of question.
I mean, the tenseness to protect yourself against possible accusation-- there was enough accusation.
Well these Jews just did it for, for the perform-- they're really not Lutherans, you know.
It was so intense that there was no Jewish life, so far as I remember it.
No way.
I mean, there was war.
This is 1942, '43, you know, the height of the Germans.
And so the idea of leaving was totally out of question.
That's right.

It was quite clear, after the Russian advance started, after Stalingrad and started moving west-- it was quite clear by the early of 1944 that it was just a question of time when Germany will collapse.
And so in 1944, the underground-- and there were two undergrounds.

There was a western underground and there was a communist underground run from Moscow-- one from London, one from Moscow-- started planning a way of bailing out out of the-- out of the Nazi dominance and finding some way of letting the Soviet army sweep west without destroying everything.
And of course, one of the issues my father had-- well, when the war really comes in, they will kill all the Jews regardless whether they are Lutheran or not anyway.
And then, then perhaps you will get killed not because you're a Jew but because the civilian population took enormous casualties.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
This was a plan which was largely launched by and supported by OSS.
And there were some OSS agents who started dropping in in early in 1944.

My father, who controlled much of the food business was party to a major move to move stocks, stocks, food stocks into the mountain, you know, under the notion that there are some, some stores out there that really need lots of inventory because you know, the roads are muddy and, you know, and God knows what.
No, absolutely not.
Absolutely didn't not know what he was doing.
Oh, he knew what he was doing.
Oh, he was-- well, he was a reserve officer of the Czechoslovak army.
You know, he was in uniform when we mobilized at the time of Munich.
Wait a minute.
Czechoslovakia mobilized in the time of Munich.
It was ready to go and fight.
So my father wore a uniform in 1938.
So you must understand my father always maintained those connections.
And so all planning for an uprising started in March of 1944.
I, of course, had no idea about it whatsoever.

A meeting took place late in June of 1944 when some of the key generals of the Slovak army decided that they are going to trigger off an uprising and execute the military mission-- let the Russians across the-- across the Carpathian Mountain passes.
There are only three passes through which you really can come in any kind of a massive army with tanks.
It's, it's, it's a mountainous region.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Communist party was given the job because this was going to be a joint action to maintain the liaison with the Soviet high command-- which by the way was commanded by Khrushchev-- to plan the takeover and plan the coup.
The Communist party absolutely did not want to have an uprising by the army because that really means that when they come over there is a nationalist army who are largely beholden to the west.
This is very much the same scenario as what happened in Poland by the way.
And so, so you have then this plan going on.
The short story is that the uprising starts prematurely in August 28, 1944.
The-- my father knew that the uprising would be happening, although he did not know exactly the date.

And so, I think in mid-August we went into our executed plan, which is go to a certain village up in the mountains and disperse the family and then just hunker down, put on pleasant clothing and so forth.
We were in that mode for about 10 days, like three days before the uprising.
And nothing was happening.
And everybody entrenched would say well, you know, where is Strassman?
You know, he's disappeared, you know.
Come back.
And so my family came back on the-- I think on the 25th or the 26th of August.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By that time of course we blew that escape plan number one.

On the night of the 27th of August, a Russian squad stopped a German train that was going from Berlin to Bucharest in a small railroad station where you put water into the tenders in order to go over the mountains, the Carpathian Mountains.
It was a small squad.
I happened to know everybody on that squad because I met them su-- subsequently.
It was largely a Russian squad.
They stopped a train while it was being refilled with water.
And to their surprise, they found that the sleeping cars were full of German general officers who were on their way from a staff meeting in Berlin.
So they took them out, shot them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The next morning, the Gestapo tried to, to implement their plan, namely disarm the Slovak army because they felt that this was a signal for the uprising.
Well, the Slovak army didn't want to be disarmed and overwhelmed the Nazis.
And the military just fled to the mountains, and the guerrillas just went, said, ah, this is the uprising.
And they started executing the uprising plan.
When this is happening, the Gestapo locally starts taking hostages.
Everyone gets taken.
My father gets taken as a hostage.
I was-- that night, purely by happenstance, sleeping at the house of a friend.
I mean, this was not planned.
I don't remember exactly why, but it was just-- you know, things were crowed.
There were other people in our house.
And my mother just said, well why don't you go and sleep with your friend?
And I stayed.
In the morning my father and my grandfather was gone.
My mother went into hiding.
We had a second plan.
This was an urban hiding plan.
In other words, we had a rural hiding plan, and we had an urban hiding plan.
We executed the urban hiding plan.
I went to the house of a trusted employee.
By the 29th of August, the Germans started executing reprisals and announced if they find any partisans or Jews hidden, they will execute everybody in the family.
The wife of this trusted employee sort of started negotiating, well, during the day, during the searches take place, why don't you go and hide yourself?
She didn't want to be executed if they found you in her house.
That's right.
And I was in the attic, of course.
I was in that house maybe two days-- two nights to be precise, two nights.
One thing I learned in that period is that I don't temporize.
I don't negotiate.
In other words, I don't try to see how-- can I temporize a little longer?
Maybe this will be acceptable.
Maybe that will be acceptable.
When they told me in the morning, why don't you go hide next to the river and come back in the evening because we don't want you during the day while the searches take place.
I went to the river.
I sat there for a couple of hours, and I heard shooting in the mountains.
And I said, that's where I want to be, because if they're shooting in the mountain, that means that the partisans are there.
And that's where my odyssey to the partisan began.
Never saw him again.
Went into hiding and she was betrayed in the hiding.
And she got captured and sent to Belsen-- Bergen-Belsen.
Oh, the 27th of August, just before she said go and sleep, you know, at your friend's house.
That's right.
My sister went into hiding in Bratislava.
In other words, rather than staying in Trencin, she went to the major town where there was a friend.
And she lasted for about 40 days.
And she was betrayed and also shipped to concentration camp.
The name of the river is Vah, V-A-H.
It flowed through the-- through the town.
And the Germans had cut off all the bridges and, and put up checkpoints to make sure that nothing moves in and out because a partisan group was already present in the vicinity.
This is first or second of September, 1944.
It's still warm.
All I have is sandals, short pants, and a t-shirt.
That's all I have.
And a belt.
That's all I have.
And underpants.
Oh, I went with a sandwich and a peach to the river in the morning.
That's all I had.
So I ate that, and that was it.
All by myself.
See lots of military action going over the-- over the roads.
I know the area very well because, you know, I swam and hiked through this entire area.
My father saw to it that I had a great deal of knowledge of the topography and you know, I was very much experienced in hiking, particularly with Hashomer Hatzair and with my father.
The town and the surroundings.
I knew the mountains and all the trails.
My father took me.
My father took me and my whole family because we had not only a primary rule retreat.
We had a secondary and a tertiary because my father had all of these lieutenants, but they were dispersed in the villages, in the circumference of 25 kilometers of Trencin.
Oh, absolutely familiar-- absolutely familiar, and including survival techniques.
You know, what mushrooms you eat and how you can dig up, for instance, dandelion and eat the roots.
Hashomer Hatzair, the survival techniques, you know, what you can eat.
In fact, you can scrounge fairly good food just if you know what you do.
And so I went into the river, which bypassed all the checkpoints, swam couple, couple of kilometers, naked with my pants wrapped around my head, you know, with a belt.
When I was out of the way of Trencin, I just got out of the river and started walking in the direction of a mountain called Inovec.
Inovec is the tallest mountain in the-- in the Trencin district.
I knew the mountain very well, all the trails, and started moving in that direction because that's where the shooting, off and on, could be heard by distance.
I went to the cottage of a forester who my people-- my parents knew.
Got some food.
Of course, the forester didn't want to keep me in the house, but at least it was a base from which to get food and get news about things.
And so during the night I came in and slept in a hayrick.
And during the day, I was wandering through the mountains.
On the second day of wandering around, I came across a small squad of Slovak soldiers who were on their way to, to, to the partisans, to join the partisans.
I came upon them and said, you know, I would like to join the partisans.
And their reaction was first, you're a kid.
Second you're a Jew.
Third, you don't know how to shoot.
You know, until then I never shot a rifle.
Never, never had arms in my hands.
And so they sort of had pity on me.
So they gave me a hand grenade.
And I said, well, what do you do with a hand grenade?
Well, here's a pin and you unroll it and then you just hold it for a while.
If you hold it too long it will blow up in your face.
But it has a four second fuse, so you have to decide when to throw it.
And it's pretty, pretty deadly stuff.
So here I have now a hand grenade on the 2nd of September, continue two more nights wandering through the mountain-- still on the Inovecs mountain.
On a second day, going through the woods, looking for food, picking berries, I see in the distance a tall man carrying something that looks like a gun.
But it was a big thing.
And so I crouched behind a tree, take the pin out of the hand grenade.
And the man passes me, and I go behind him, hold the hand grenade behind his neck and say, I want your gun.
The guy turns around-- it happens to be a friend of my father's from the army-- a Jew, Kartal-- a tall man.
He apparently got somehow a Bren gun-- a Bren gun is a light machine gun-- somewhere.
And he was on his way to join the partisans.
He said, Strassman, put that pin in because if you're going to kill both of us.
And so I joined Kartal.
Forget about the forester.
And so two of us, one with a light machine gun, me with my hand grenade started going now decisively away from the Inovecs, going east, whereby this time you could hear artillery.
We come down and go through several mountain passes.
And then we see-- observe from the edge of the forest, a truck.
And there are these guys in civilian clothes, but heavily armed, who are in the process of boarding the, the truck.
We walk over there, and they are partisans.
And what they need is a light machine gun to put on top of the truck housing.
And, and so they take Kartal and me to headquarters where we get debriefed.
Kartal decides that he really wants to go to headquarters.
So he goes further east where the partisan headquarters was.
I liked these, these-- this commando squad.
It happens to be a commando squad that was run by parachutist-- a professional partisan by name of Bat'ko, a Russian, who's left hand, in fact, has been amputated.
But he has a Russian round cylinder machine gun, and he keeps the light machine gun.
He has a squad of eight.
And it turned out that his orders were not to engage with the-- with the Russians-- with, with the Germans.
See, the Slovak army wants to hold land until the Russians come over the Carpathian Mountains.
Of course, the Russians have no intention of doing that.
So the Slovak army occupies 60 by 200 kilometer piece of land.
They declare a Slovak Republic on the third or fourth of September of 1944.
They elect a president.
They create a legislature.
They go through the whole rigmarole.
In fact, they capture some airfield and both American allied airplanes land and Russian airplanes land there.
There is a brigade of Slovaks who are now fighting with the Russians who get transported to that airfield to strengthen hold at republic.
Their republic, of course, is doomed.
By October 28 the Germans, with overwhelming force, overran it.
OK.
